coredata.dc:title: A facile photothermometric sensor of acid phosphatase based on CoOOH nanozymes-mediated 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine photothermal system
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Kongxiang
Rao, Honghong
Xue, Xin
Luo, Mingyue
Xue, Zhonghua
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) reaction systems have been extensively used for fabricating colorimetric sensors, but they still require advanced instruments like color analysis software or ultraviolet spectrophotometers to achieve accurate quantitative analysis. Herein, we explored an alternative strategy to convert a color readout to a temperature readout of a CoOOH nanoflakes-TMB colorimetric reaction system, and successfully fabricated a simple and facile photothermometric sensor of acid phosphatase (ACP) by using a common thermometer as the reader. It mainly performed based on the strong photothermal effect of the oxidation product (TMBox) of TMB upon an 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation, which showed a close relationship with the ACP content with a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.037 mU/mL for photothermometric ACP determination. This photothermometric method was proved to be suitable for not only sensing ACP but also screening its inhibitor in human serum, and providing a simple, rapid, convenient, low cost, and portable organic chromogenic substrate-based sensing platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine
CoOOH nanozymes
Colorimetric
Photothermometric sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conditions optimization of the CoOOH-TMB sensing system for ACP detection
Colorimetric and photothermometric detection of AA
Colorimetric and photothermometric detection of ACP
ACP detection in human serum sample
Feasibility of colorimetric and photothermometric detection of ACP
Colorimetric and photothermometric detection of AA and ACP
Selectivity of the proposed sensing system for ACP detection
Investigation of the proposed sensing system for ACP inhibitor screening
Applicability assessment of the proposed sensing system for ACP detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key talent project of Gansu province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22064014 , 21765013 ), and the Key talent project of Gansu province ( 2019-115 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the as-synthesized CoOOH NFs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectra (Inset: the corresponding photograph), (B) and (C) TEM image, (D) Particle size distribution diagram.

Characterization of CoOOH NFs. (A) TEM image after the addition of AA, (B) FT-IR spectrum, (C) XPS spectrum after the addition of AA, (D) UV–vis spectrum. Inset: (a), (b) represent before and after the addition of AA, respectively.

(A) UV–vis spectrum of the sensing system upon the addition of ACP with the different concentrations ranging from 0 to 0.750 mU/mL and the corresponding color photographs (insert), (B) Relationship between the absorbance of the system at 652 nm and the ACP concentration with a linear range from 0.025 to 0.750 mU/mL. (C) Relationship between the temperature change values (ΔT = T − T0) of the system and the ACP concentration with a linear range from 0.050 to 0.750 mU/mL.
Investigation on the selectivity of the proposed sensing system. (A) Characteristic UV–vis absorbance of A652 nm, and (B) Temperature changes (ΔT) in the presence of different interferences.
Schematic illustration of the CoOOH-TMB sensing system for ACP detection.
ACP detection in human serum samples based on the standard addition method.

head.ce:title: A facile photothermometric sensor of acid phosphatase based on CoOOH nanozymes-mediated 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine photothermal system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou City University, Lanzhou 730070, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou City University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We explored an alternative strategy to convert a color readout to a temperature readout of a CoOOH nanoflakes-TMB colorimetric reaction system, and successfully fabricated a simple and facile photothermometric sensor of acid phosphatase (ACP) by using a common thermometer as the reader.

Nanozymes-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) reaction systems have been extensively used for fabricating colorimetric sensors, but they still require advanced instruments like color analysis software or ultraviolet spectrophotometers to achieve accurate quantitative analysis. Herein, we explored an alternative strategy to convert a color readout to a temperature readout of a CoOOH nanoflakes-TMB colorimetric reaction system, and successfully fabricated a simple and facile photothermometric sensor of acid phosphatase (ACP) by using a common thermometer as the reader. It mainly performed based on the strong photothermal effect of the oxidation product (TMBox) of TMB upon an 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation, which showed a close relationship with the ACP content with a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.037 mU/mL for photothermometric ACP determination. This photothermometric method was proved to be suitable for not only sensing ACP but also screening its inhibitor in human serum, and providing a simple, rapid, convenient, low cost, and portable organic chromogenic substrate-based sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:para: The organic chromogenic substrate of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was attracted great attention since the discovery of Fe3O4 nanozymes in 2007 [1]. Lots of colorimetric sensing methods were explored mainly relying on the deep blue color of the oxidation product of TMB (TMBox) that catalyzed by the various nanozymes [2,3]. More significantly, the adjustable properties of nanozyme activity enable such kind of colorimetric methods more potential in different sensing events involving environmental monitor [4], food analysis [5], medical diagnosis [6], and many other fields [7]. Though many successes have been made, these nanozymes-TMB based colorimetric still required advanced instruments such as color analysis software or ultraviolet spectrophotometers to achieve accurate quantitative analysis, which further limits its potential applications [8–10]. Therefore, it is important to develop an alternative way especially with a more simple yet sensitive readout to achieve the accurate quantitative analysis but avoiding the use of precise instruments or complex operations.

More recently, the good photothermal effect of TMBox as the other side nature compared to its color characteristic has attracted more attention to the researchers. Li et al. developed a simple, portable, and accurate temperature readout-based bioassay by using the strong photothermal feature of TMBox under the near-infrared laser-driven for the first time [11]. Inspired by this creative work, lots of efforts have been made to successfully convert the color readout to the heat (temperature) readout to achieve different photothermometric sensors, as the TMBox has two-faced characteristics of the deep blue color and the sensitive photothermal effect. By using such simple signal conversion, the simple, portable yet accurate analysis could be easily realized without the assistance of the instrument-based detecting reader. It is of great significance for point-of-care testing (POCT) and practical detection in resource-poor areas [12,13]. As the research progressed, it was also found that TMBox can often be obtained in a nanozymes-TMB reaction system, even in a very convenient and rapid manner [14,15]. Such feature further enables this kind of photothermometric method demonstrating many advantages, such as good simplicity, wide diversity, practical operability and ease to design. For example, by using the tunability of nanozymes activity [16], biomarker [8], and glucose [9] were successfully detected through a common thermometer.

Although considerable progress has been made in nanozymes-TMB based photothermometric fields, the application in photothermal detection using TMBox as the heat indicator (photothermal probe) is still in its infancy. Particularly, combining the specific recognition of target analytes with the regulation of nanozymes activity to develop a simple, facile, and portable photothermometric method is of great importance for highly on-site recognition applications especially for the bioanalysis related to disease diagnosis. Based on this consideration, we chose CoOOH nanoflakes (CoOOH NFs) and used its high oxidase-like catalytic activity to produce a photothermal probe of TMBox [17]. Then we selected acid phosphatase (ACP), a disease marker closely related to several diseases including malignant tumor bone [18], prostate cancer [19], more gauche disease [20], hyperparathyroidism acute [21] and chronic myelogenous leukemia [22] as the target model to integrate with the flexible modulation of CoOOH NFs nanozymes catalytic activity, and finally to construct a new photothermometric strategy with a simple temperature readout for ACP detection. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the ACP activity detection by using a common thermometer as the reader on the basis of CoOOH-TMB reaction system.

In this work, we prepared CoOOH NFs nanozymes and found it could directly catalyze the oxidation of colorless TMB to a deep blue TMBox product in the CoOOH-TMB reaction system [17]. Such fabricated system then could exhibit a significant thermal response signal output upon an 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser-driven, which may be simply measured by only using a common thermometer. Such photothermal system may exhibit the sensitive heat response toward ACP introduction. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the corresponding sensing principle is based on the fact that ACP hydrolyzed its substrate of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce ascorbic acid (AA) [23], and the generated AA further triggered decomposition of CoOOH NFs and resulted in the significant decrease of CoOOH NFs nanozymes catalytic activity [24]. The contents of TMBox are closely related to the catalytic activity of CoOOH NFs nanozymes in such sensing system thus also exhibited an ACP content-dependent feature, accompanied by the significant color change from deep blue to light blue as well as the dramatic high-to-low temperature change with the increasing ACP concentrations. In summary, colorimetric and photothermometric sensing platform for ACP detection could be successfully constructed on the basis of this proposed CoOOH-TMB reaction system. It is worth noting that photothermometric strategy demonstrated good simplicity and high resolution over the colorimetric one due to the recommendation of the daily used thermometer. Furthermore, the proposed method also could be utilized for the screening of ACP inhibitor, further indicating its good diversity in practical applications.

Ascorbic acid phosphate (AAP) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Acid phosphatase (ACP) was obtained from Yuanye Biotech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ascorbic acid (AA) was offered from Shanghai Zhongqin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4), HAc, NaAc were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The interferences including horseradish peroxidase (HRP), catalase (CAT), hyaluronidase (HAase), uricase (UAO), glucose oxidase (GOx), Lysozyme (LZM), bovine serum albumin (BSA), ascorbate oxidase (AAO), superoxide dismutase (SOD), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Human serum sample of the healthy volunteers was collected from the Hospital 940 of PLA Joint Logistics Support Force (Gansu, China). Ultrapure water (>18.24 MΩ.cm) was used to prepare all reagents in all experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectra wasrecorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (T6 new century, China). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was surveyed on an ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) morphology was obtained using an FEI Tecnai F20 (200 kV) instrument equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray detector (EDX) (Gatan832, EDAX). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was monitored using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6000 Shimadzu). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on a FTS3000 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (DIGI-LAB, USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. An 808 nm diode laser (MD L-H-808-5W-17120005) with an output power from 0 to 5.0 W was purchased from Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co. Ltd (China). The incubation of ACP was performed by a Jing Hong DK-S24 constant warm water bath (China). A pen-style digital thermometer (MITIR-TP677) was purchased from a local supermarket. The 96-well plates were purchased from Greiner Bio-One. All photos were taken with a Canon camera (EOS-70D).

CoOOH NFs were synthesized via a redox reaction between cobalt chloride and sodium hypochlorite according to previous reports that were prepared according to a typical procedure with minor modification [25–28]. 2 mL of NaOH solution (1.0 M) was mixed with 50 mL of CoCl2·6H2O solution (10 mM) with vigorous stirring. After sonicated for 5 min, 2.0 mL of NaClO solution (0.9 M) was added, and then the mixture was kept to be sonicated for 20 min. After that, the resultant solution of CoOOH NFs was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. Then the collected precipitates were washed three times with the ultrapure water. The obtained brownish-black CoOOH NFs were redispersed in 250 mL of ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conditions optimization of the CoOOH-TMB sensing system for ACP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of CoOOH NFs and TMB, pH, the reaction time, and the irradiation time of the proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system was investigated by using UV–vis spectra. The characteristic absorbance of TMBox at 652 nm as the criterion was proposed to optimize the above reaction conditions. Since the photothermometric sensing in this approach was performed based on the color change of the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system, the optimization of the conditions were consistent with this process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and photothermometric detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para:
80 μL of CoOOH NFs (0.121 mg/mL) and 100 μL of AA solution with various concentrations were added to 1770 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.200 M, pH 4.0). Then 50 μL of TMB aqueous solution (0.010 M) was added into the above solution to incubate for 15 min at the room temperature. After that, UV–vis absorption spectra of the above reaction system was recorded to colorimetric sensing AA. As far as the photothermometric detection of AA, the above colorimetric reaction solutions were irradiated horizontally with an 808 nm laser at a power of 4.5 W for 80 s, and meantime monitored the temperature changes of the reaction system by using a pen-style digital thermometer. To clearly indicate such temperature change of the colorimetric system, we denoted the temperature variation by ΔT according to the following equation:
(1) ΔT (°C) = T1 − T0
where ΔT is the temperature variation of the colorimetric system, T0 and T1 refer to the stable temperature before and after laser irradiation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and photothermometric detection of ACP
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100 μL of AAP (0.005 M) and 100 μL of ACP with different concentrations were added into 100 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.200 M, pH 5.0). Then, the solution were incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. Subsequently, 80 μL of CoOOH NFs (0.121 mg/mL), 1570 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.200 M, pH 4.0), and 50 μL of TMB solution (0.010 M) were added to the above solution to react at room temperature for 15 min. After the color change of the solution system is stable, observing the color of the reaction system with the naked eye and recording the UV–vis absorption spectra by UV–vis spectrophotometer to colorimetric sensing ACP. For the photothermometric detection of ACP, the operation was the same as the above mentioned procedure of photothermometric AA determination. Similarly, the temperature change (ΔT) of the corresponding colorimetric system was proposed to sense ACP as the photothermal readout by using a pen-style digital thermometer as the reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ACP detection in human serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: ACP level in a healthy human serum sample is lower and it often is difficult to directly detect. So we used the standard addition method to evaluate the potential applicability of the proposed colorimetric and photothermometric method as using the human serum as the testing sample. Firstly, the obtained serum sample was diluted 10 times with ultrapure water. Then the solution containing 20 μL of diluted serum (10%) was gradually added 0.100, 0.300, and 0.600 mU/mL ACP, respectively. Then 100 μL of AAP (0.005 M) and 100 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.200 M, pH 5.0) were added into the above solution, respectively. After incubating at 37 °C for 60 min, 80 μL of CoOOH NFs (0.121 mg/mL), 1550 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (0.200 M, pH 4.0) and 50 μL of TMB solution (0.010 M) were gradually added into the above reaction solution to react at room temperature for 15 min. Finally, the absorbance and temperature changes in the sensing system were measured.

CoOOH NFs prepared in this work were confirmed by using a wide variety of characterization techniques including UV–vis spectra, TEM, XRD, EDX, XPS, and FTIR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, UV–vis spectra display a characteristic absorbance of the prepared CoOOH NFs samples at 398 nm in the UV–vis spectral region along with a light brown color, indicating a sharp contrast to the light pink colored CoCl2 solution with a characteristic absorbance at 512 nm. TEM images (Fig. 1B-D) reveal that the obtained CoOOH NFs have a typical hexagonal nanoflakes morphology with an average diameter of 66.59 nm and an average thickness of 9.2 nm. The CoOOH XRD pattern displayed in Fig. S1A shows three characteristic crystalline peaks that correspond to the (003), (012), and (018) at 20.04°, 39.02°, and 50.38°, respectively, which are in good agreement with the JCPDS standard card (No. 07-0169), further proving the successful preparation of CoOOH [17,25]. All above results are well consistent with the previous reports. Furthermore, the presence of cobalt and oxygen in the CoOOH NFs sample was measured by EDX (Fig. S1B) and XPS (Fig. S1C), which further proves the successful preparation of the material. It is worth noting that the presence of copper elements in EDX is due to our use of lacey support films as substrates, which contain copper in their grids. As shown in Fig. S1D, two typical peaks located at 795.6 eV (Co 2p1/2) and 780.2 eV (Co 2p3/2) could be clearly observed in the Co 2p XPS spectra.20, 27 Also, three characteristic peaks from the curve-fitted Co 2p3/2 spectra at 780.2, 781.3, and 790.4 eV further indicate the existence of Co (III) in the prepared CoOOH NFs. From the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1F), it is clear that the characteristic peaks for the typical groups in CoOOH NFs are well presented, including the symmetric stretching of hydroxyl bond (–OH) at 3438 cm−1, the O–H bond at 2926 cm−1, the Co–O double bond at 1639 cm−1 and the Co–O2− vibration at 585 cm−1 [25,29–31]. Besides, the XPS of O 1s in Fig S1 (E). In O 1s spectrum, three kinds of bonds are deconvolved, corresponding to the Co-OH bond (530.82 eV), oxygen vacancy (531.67 eV), and Co-O bond (529.6 eV) [32,33]. In summary, all these results strongly confirm the successful preparation of CoOOH NFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of colorimetric and photothermometric detection of ACP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system for colorimetric and photothermometric detecting ACP, we investigate the color change and UV–vis absorbance variation as well as the photothermal response of the proposed sensing system upon the addition of ACP. As clearly, an obvious deep blue color (Fig. S3A) along with two distinct UV–vis absorption peak at 652 nm and 890 nm (Fig. S3B) were clearly observed for the CoOOH-TMB reaction system. It may be attributed to the effective oxidation of the colorless TMB to form its oxidation product (TMBox) that was catalyzed by the CoOOH NFs nanozymes. As a color readout generation, it indicates that using CoOOH NFs nanozymes we can easily obtain the colorimetric signal with an obvious “turn-on” feature. But for the control group system such as individual CoOOH NFs and TMB, no obvious color and UV–vis absorption change were observed, confirming the possibility of the above CoOOH NFs-catalyzed TMB oxidation process. Further observations were also presented to evaluate the feasibility of this CoOOH-TMB system toward ACP detection. Firstly, AA was added into this CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system. It was found that the deep blue color of the reaction system faded to light blue, accompanied by dramatic decreases both on the characteristic UV–vis absorption peak at 652 nm, and 890 nm. These results suggest that AA could effectively weaken the catalytic activity of CoOOH NFs nanozymes, and resulting in the reduction of TMBox generation. Such observations further demonstrate a high probability for the proposed CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system to sense ACP. Because we all know that ACP can directly induce the production of AA by exclusively hydrolyzing its substrate of AAP. As a reliable verification, we also observed the nearly identical changes in the color and UV–vis absorbance for the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system with the addition of ACP and AAP, comparing to the reaction system of AA addition. All the above observations strongly indicate that our proposed CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system has an obvious color and absorbance response toward ACP, indicating good feasibility for colorimetric sensing ACP.

But for the highly accurate analysis of ACP with this colorimetric method, the color analysis software or the ultraviolet spectrophotometers usually are necessary. To address this limitation, we attempted to seek another signal readout that could be easily and accurately detected by using a portable reader. Taking the benefit of the strong photothermal response of TMBox in the NIR region, therefore, we investigated the photothermal response of the above colorimetric system. As displayed in Fig. 2 C, the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system displayed a dramatic photo-to-heat response with a significant temperature increase upon an 808 nm diode laser irradiation for 80 s. Such increased temperature in the above colorimetric system was observed could be inhibited by the addition of AA. Similarly, with the addition of AAP and ACP, the same change on the increased temperature of the proposed CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system was also observed. All these observations suggest that this colorimetric system also has a sensitive photothermal response toward ACP, thus indicating a good possibility for photothermometric sensing ACP.

To further confirm the sensing mechanism of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system to ACP detection, we investigated the possible intermediate substances produced in the proposed reaction system by using UV–vis spectrophotometer. As well know that the core sensing principle in this CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system could be elucidated in the following two aspects. One is the high catalytic activity of the CoOOH NFs nanozymes, which usually exhibited good performance to oxidize chromogenic substrate to produce color change as the sensing readout. Another is the TMB indicator, as an extensively used organic chromogenic substrate, which can be rapidly and stably oxidized to produce a deep blue color product (TMBox).

As far as the investigation of the catalytic activity of CoOOH NFs nanozymes, many efforts have been explored that some reactive oxide species (ROS) often play a key role in the nanozyme catalysis, such as O2−, 1O2 and OH. So we studied the possible situations of the above ROS in our sensing system. Fig. S4A shows the UV–vis spectra of the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system upon the different radical scavengers. It is clear that only the presence of the superoxide radical (SOD) and NaN3 induced the sensing system showing an obvious UV–vis spectra change along with a significant decrease in the absorbance of TMBox at 652 nm (Fig. S4B) and an obvious deep-to-light blue color change (not shown). No obvious change was observed on the UV–vis spectra and the system color for other radical scavengers such as mannitol, T-butano, and NaHCO3. These observations suggest that O2− and 1O2 active species were generated in the proposed sensing system, but no OH was existing in such system. Further investigations also were performed to study the effect of the different radical scavengers such as SOD and NaN3 on the characteristic UV–vis absorption peak of TMBox at 652 nm. As shown in Fig. S4C, the addition of 0.2 mM and 0.3 mM SOD both induced a gradually decrease in the characteristic TMBox absorbance with a A652 nm of 1.12 (0.2 mM SOD) and A652 nm of 0.96 (0.3 mM SOD), respectively. However, with the increasing SOD content such as 0.4 and 0.5 mM, no obvious continued decreased absorbance was observed. Fig. S4D displays the change of the characteristic TMBox absorbance upon the increasing NaN3 concentrations (0.3, 0.4, and 0.6 mM) [14,16,34,35]. It is clear that a distinct decrease in characteristic A652 nm was observed upon the addition of increased NaN3 concentrations with a A652 nm of 0.72 (0.3 mM NaN3), A652 nm of 0.65 (0.4 mM NaN3), and A652 nm of 0.47 (0.4 mM NaN3), respectively. From all above observations, we think that 1O2 as the dominant species than O2− in our proposed sensing system to oxidize TMB to produce the colorimetric and photothermometric indicator of TMBox.

Based on these observations, we all believe the CoOOH NFs nanozymes in our system was performed as the oxidase-like activity, by which the color and temperature sensing readout with significant “turn-on” feature could be successfully provided. With the addition of AA or the introduction of the ACP and AAP reaction system into the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system, the observed color and UV–vis spectra, as well as photothermal response as shown in Fig. 2, may be attributed to the inhibition of the oxidase-like catalytic activity of CoOOH NFs nanozymes. It may be attributed to the fact that AA with reducing feature can react with CoOOH NFs nanozymes, leading to the destruction of CoOOH NFs nanozymes and the its resultant decrease on the oxidase-like activity. To further confirm it, we investigated the reaction system of the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system in the presence of AA by using TEM, FT-IR, XPS, and UV–vis. As shown in Fig. 2A, only very few hexagonal CoOOH NFs was observed for the proposed sensing system with the addition of AA, indicating an obvious etching performance of AA toward CoOOH NFs. FT-IR spectrum shown in Fig. 2B displays that the Co-O peak at 1639 cm−1 of CoOOH NFs was disappeared after AA addition, indicating an effective reaction between AA and CoOOH NFs. Also, this can be cross-corroborated from the XPS spectrum as shown in Fig. 2C, in which the peak of Co 2p was greatly decreased after the AA introduction compared to that of CoOOH NFs (Fig. S1C). Finally, UV–vis spectrum also suggested that AA could induce a disappeared characteristic absorption of CoOOH NFs at 398 nm and the corresponding solution color change from yellowish to near-colorless. All these observations suggest that AA could induce the decomposition of CoOOH NFs nanozymes, and further resulted in the significant inhibition on its oxidase-like activity [36–38]. It then provides an opportunity to produce a sensing readout with a distinct “on-to-off” characteristic, strongly supporting the possibilities of the proposed colorimetric and photothermometric sensing platform not only for AA but also for ACP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and photothermometric detection of AA and ACP
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to study the colorimetric and photothermometric sensing performance of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system toward ACP, the optimal experimental conditions such as TMB concentration, CoOOH NFs concentration, pH, reaction time, and irradiation time of the system were investigated carefully, respectively. Due to the core point of our sensing system is the readout change related to the TMBox production, so its characteristic UV–vis absorbance of A652 nm is chosen as the criteria to optimize the above mentioned experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. S1, pH 4.0, the reaction time 15 min and 0.25 mM of TMB could lead to a maximum A652 nm for the reaction system, respectively. Therefore, they were chosen as the optimal parameters in the subsequent experiments. For the content of CoOOH NFs, it is clear that A652 nm of the reaction system was observed to be gradually increasing with the increasing CoOOH NFs concentration. Considering the economic effects, we selected 4.8 μg/mL as the optimal CoOOH NFs concentration to perform this study. For the photothermometric investigations, it is well known that the irradiation times of an 808 nm diode laser toward the colorimetric system has a significant influence on the sensing performance. So the photothermometric performance of the sensing system upon different CoOOH NFs concentrations was investigated at the different irradiation times, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S5, the sensing system showed a gradual temperature increasing tendency with the increasing CoOOH NFs concentrations. At a fixed content of CoOOH NFs, this observed system temperature also gradually increased with the increasing irradiation times from 30 to 160 s. Among that, we observed that this obtained temperature elevation has a good proportional to the logarithm of the CoOOH NFs contents at 80 s than others irradiation times. Thus, we selected 80 s as the optimal irradiation time in the subsequent study.

Based on the above optimized conditions, the sensing performance of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system toward AA and ACP detection was investigated through a colorimetric and photothermometric method, respectively. It is seen from Fig. S6A that the absorbance of the sensing system in the UV–vis spectra at 652 nm and 890 nm gradually decreased with the increasing AA concentrations along with an obvious deep-to-light blue color change. In a range of 7.5 to 45.0 μM, the A652 nm of the sensing system was proportional to the AA concentration (Fig. S6B) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 4.2 μM (S/N = 3), suggesting an acceptable sensing performance of our proposed colorimetric sensing system for AA detection. But this colorimetric sensing performance should be obtained by the assistance of ultraviolet spectrophotometer. To achieve the analysis with the same performance but with a simple, rapid, and cost-effective way. We subsequently investigated the sensing performance of the proposed photothermometric method developed based on the above CoOOH-TMB reaction system, using an 808 nm near-infrared (NIR) laser as the photothermal excitation source and a common pressure meter as the reader. As shown in Fig. S6C, the temperature elevation of the photothermal reaction system (ΔT) dramatically decreased with the increasing AA concentration. The measured ΔT was proportional to the AA contents in a range of 2.5 to 42.5 μM, and along with a LOD of 4.5 μM (S/N = 3). Though the LOD by the photothermometric method is comparable to the colorimetric strategy, the measurement for the temperature readout was easily and simply achieved by using a common thermometer reader, enabling this proposed photothermometric method more simple, and portable in the different sensing applications.

Similarly, we studied the sensing performance of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system toward ACP detection. The specific operations are the same as the detection of AA upon the above optimal experimental conditions. As clearly from Fig. 3 A, the characteristic UV–vis absorption peak of TMBox at 652 nm and 890 nm decreased gradually with the increasing ACP concentration, accompanied by an obvious ACP content-dependent color change of the reaction system from deep blue to light blue. It was also found that the A652 nm of the reaction system was proportional to the ACP concentration in the range from 0 to 0.750 mU/mL (Fig. 3B) with a good linear relationship of 0.025 to 0.750 mU/mL. The calculated LOD for ACP by this colorimetric method was 0.021 mU/mL. For the photothermometric sensing of ACP, the same observations could be observed from Fig. 3C. The T of the reaction system also significantly decreased with the increasing ACP, and which was proportional to the ACP contents in a range of 0.050 to 0.750 mU/mL, along with a LOD of 0.037 mU/mL (S/N = 3). All these results demonstrated a good performance of our developed sensing system toward ACP detection compared with other methods (Table S1) both by using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer and a common thermometer. But considering the practicality, photothermal based on the temperature readout is more advantageous than the color readout based method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed sensing system for ACP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system for ACP detection, some biological substances as the interferences were used for examining their influence on the detection of the target ACP, such as HRP, CAT, HAase, UAO, GOx, LZM, ALP and AChE. All these interferences were recommended with the same concentration of 15.000 mU/mL. In addition, BSA with a concentration of 0.250 mg/mL as another important interference was also proposed. As displayed in Fig. 4 , only the addition of target ACP (0.750 mU/mL) induced a significant decrease on the characteristic UV–vis absorbance of A652 nm and a distinct inhibition on the increased temperature change (ΔT) of the CoOOH-TMB sensing system. No significant characteristic UV–vis absorbance and increased temperature change was observed for the addition of the tested interferences. These results indicate that this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system has an excellent selectivity toward ACP determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the proposed sensing system for ACP inhibitor screening
ce:section.ce:para:
As well known, most developed enzyme sensors often exhibit an effective screening function toward the corresponding inhibitor of enzyme. We thus investigated the screening performance of this developed sensing system with the proposed colorimetric method by using Na2MoO4 as a typical inhibitor of ACP [39]. As shown in Fig. S7A, with the increasing Na2MoO4 content, the gradually increased characteristic UV–vis absorbance of A652 nm along with an obvious light-to-deep blue color change in the sensing system were clearly observed. This result indicates a significant inhibition effect of Na2MoO4 on ACP activity. The inhibition efficiency (IE) of Na2MoO4 toward ACP activity could be expressed by the following equation:
IE (%) = 100*(Ai − A)/(A0 − A)
where A0 and A is the A652 nm of the proposed sensing system in the presence of AAP and the presence of AAP and ACP, respectively. Ai is above A652 nm but in the presence of AAP, ACP and Na2MoO4. It was found that the IE was proportional to the Na2MoO4 concentration in the range of 0.01 to 60.00 µM (Fig. S7B), indicating a good possibility of this sensing system for the screening of Na2MoO4. Based on this, the half-maximal inhibition concentration (IC50) of Na2MoO4 was calculated to be 2.51 µM, an acceptable value compared to the literature reports.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability assessment of the proposed sensing system for ACP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility and applicability of this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system, ACP content in the practical human serum samples was tested by using the standard addition method. As listed in Table 1 , the recoveries of the three spiked ACP samples (the addition of ACP is 0.100, 0.300, 0.600 mU/mL, respectively) varied from 99.5% to 102.4% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranged from 1.1% to 2.9% for the colorimetric measurement. It was found that in the photothermometric testing, the obtained recoveries varied from 100.3% to 101.1% with a RSD ranged from 1.6% to 4.9%. All these results indicate that this proposed CoOOH-TMB sensing system exhibits an acceptable reliability toward ACP determination. Here we state that all experiments related with the human serum samples were performed in accordance with the Guidelines on Administration of Lab, and approved by the ethics committee at Northwest Normal University. most developed enzyme sensors often exhibit an effective

In summary, we proposed a facile photothermometric sensor to detect ACP activity and on the basis of the CoOOH-TMB colorimetric system. As highly efficient nanozymes with good biomimetic oxidase activity, CoOOH NFs can catalyze the oxidation of TMB into TMBox along with a distinct color change from colorless to deep blue. It thus enables the possibility of this proposed colorimetric system to sense ACP by using the generated color readout. But the limitation from the demands of the color readout reader like color analysis software or ultraviolet spectrophotometers is still existing in the accurate quantitative analysis. Significantly, the good photothermal effect of TMBox in this system helped us to design an alternative to breaking the above limitation in the color readout based analysis platform. As a result, this proposed photothermometric strategy was successfully used to sense ACP with high selectivity and sensitivity. It is worth noting that our photothermometric sensing platform makes a new attempt for ACP determination based on the oxidase-like activity of CoOOH NFs, expanding the diversity of CoOOH nanozymes in the practical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22064014, 21765013), and the Key talent project of Gansu province (2019-115).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106736.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
